preamble.summary: A psalm of self-examination and protest of innocence. David appeals to God to vindicate him, declaring that he has walked in integrity, avoided the company of the wicked, loved the house of the LORD, and kept his hands clean. The psalm moves from personal examination (vv. 1-3) to separation from evildoers (vv. 4-5) to worship at the altar (vv. 6-8) to a final plea for redemption and grace (vv. 9-12).
preamble.remarkable: This psalm is uncomfortable for modern readers who associate piety with humility and self-deprecation. David does not say 'I am a sinner in need of mercy' — he says 'Vindicate me, for I have walked in integrity.' The Hebrew Bible sees no contradiction between genuine piety and honest self-assessment. The speaker is not claiming sinless perfection but covenant faithfulness — he has kept his commitments, avoided corruption, and maintained his worship. The psalm functions as a negative confession: 'I have not sat with the worthless, I have not associated with hypocrites, I hate the assembly of evildoers.' This is not arrogance but testimony, offered to the God who can verify it.
preamble.friction: The tension between this psalm and psalms like 25 (where David confesses sins) and 51 (where David begs for cleansing) is real but not contradictory. Different situations call for different postures. When falsely accused, the appropriate response is to assert innocence. When genuinely guilty, the appropriate response is to confess. The same person can do both at different times. The verb shafeteni ('vindicate me, judge me') in verse 1 is a legal term — David is asking for a court hearing, confident the evidence will support him.
preamble.connections: The protest of innocence echoes Job 31, the great oath of innocence. The love for God's house (v. 8) anticipates Psalm 27:4 ('One thing I have asked') and Psalm 84 ('How lovely is your dwelling place'). The hand-washing ritual (v. 6) connects to Deuteronomy 21:6-7 (the elders washing hands over the heifer) and to Pilate's gesture in Matthew 27:24. The 'level ground' (mishor) of verse 12 echoes the 'right paths' (magelei tsedeq) of Psalm 23:3.

verses.text_hebrew: לְדָוִ֗ד שׇׁפְטֵ֥נִי יְהוָ֗ה כִּֽי־אֲ֭נִי בְּתֻמִּ֣י הָלַ֑כְתִּי וּבַיהוָ֥ה בָּ֝טַ֗חְתִּי לֹ֣א אֶמְעָֽד׃
verses.text_kjv: Judge me, O LORD; for I have walked in mine integrity: I have trusted also in the LORD; therefore I shall not slide.
verses.rendering:
Of David.
Vindicate me, O LORD,
for I have walked in my integrity,
and I have trusted in the LORD without wavering.
verses.expanded_rendering: The opening is bold: shofeteni YHWH ('judge me, LORD'). The speaker invites God's scrutiny, confident in the verdict. The grounds: be-tummi halakhti ('I have walked in my integrity'). Tom ('integrity, completeness, blamelessness') is the same word used of Job in Job 1:1. It does not mean perfection but wholeness — a life without duplicity or hidden corruption. The second ground: ba-YHWH batachti ('in the LORD I have trusted'). The conclusion: lo emad ('I will not slip, waver, stagger'). Trust in God provides stability; integrity provides the basis for the appeal.
translator_notes: Shafat in the judicial sense means 'to render a verdict, to vindicate' — the speaker is asking for acquittal, not punishment. The confidence is remarkable: this is someone who believes the evidence supports him and wants his day in court before the ultimate judge.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּחָנֵ֣נִי יְהוָ֣ה וְנַסֵּ֑נִי צׇרְפָ֖ה כִלְיוֹתַ֣י וְלִבִּֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: Examine me, O LORD, and prove me; try my reins and my heart.
verses.rendering:
Examine me, O LORD, and test me;
refine my inner depths and my heart.
translator_notes: Three verbs of testing: bachan ('examine, investigate'), nasah ('test, try'), and tsaraf ('refine, smelt'). The third is metallurgical — tsaraf is what a silversmith does, heating metal to remove impurities. The speaker asks God to apply this process to his kilyotai ('kidneys') and libbo ('heart'). In Hebrew anthropology, the kidneys were the seat of deep emotion and conscience, while the heart was the seat of thought and will. Together they represent the entire inner life.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּי חַסְדְּךָ֥ לְנֶ֣גֶד עֵינָ֑י וְ֝הִתְהַלַּ֗כְתִּי בַּאֲמִתֶּֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: For thy lovingkindness is before mine eyes: and I have walked in thy truth.
verses.rendering:
For your faithful love is before my eyes,
and I have walked in your truth.

key_terms.semantic_range: faithful love, loyal kindness, covenant devotion, mercy, steadfast love
key_terms.note: The speaker's integrity is not self-generated but responsive — he has kept God's chesed 'before his eyes' (le-neged einai) as his constant reference point. His moral life is a response to God's covenant love, not an independent achievement.

translator_notes: The pairing of chesed and emet (amittekha, 'your truth') echoes Psalm 25:10 — the twin pillars of God's character. The speaker's walk (hithallakhti, 'I have walked about, conducted myself') is shaped by these divine attributes.

verses.text_hebrew: לֹא־יָ֭שַׁבְתִּי עִם־מְתֵי־שָׁ֑וְא וְעִ֥ם נַ֝עֲלָמִ֗ים לֹ֣א אָבֽוֹא׃
verses.text_kjv: I have not sat with vain persons, neither will I go in with dissemblers.
verses.rendering:
I have not sat with worthless people,
and with the deceitful I will not associate.
translator_notes: The negative confession begins. Lo yashavti im-metei-shav ('I have not sat with men of emptiness/falsehood') — shav means 'emptiness, worthlessness, falsehood.' These are people whose lives are hollow. Naalamim ('the hidden ones, the concealed, the dissemblers') are those who hide their true intentions — hypocrites whose public face masks private corruption. The speaker has avoided both.

verses.text_hebrew: שָׂ֭נֵאתִי קְהַ֣ל מְרֵעִ֑ים וְעִם־רְ֝שָׁעִ֗ים לֹ֣א אֵשֵֽׁב׃
verses.text_kjv: I have hated the congregation of evil doers; and will not sit with the wicked.
verses.rendering:
I hate the assembly of evildoers,
and with the wicked I will not sit.
translator_notes: The language intensifies from avoidance to hatred. Saneiti ('I have hated') is a strong verb — the speaker does not merely decline to join evildoers; he actively opposes their gatherings. Qehal mereim ('assembly of evildoers') uses the same word qahal that describes Israel's worship assembly. The evildoers have their own congregation — a parody of true worship — and the speaker refuses membership.

verses.text_hebrew: אֶרְחַ֣ץ בְּנִקָּי֣וֹן כַּפָּ֑י וַאֲסֹבְבָ֖ה אֶת־מִזְבַּחֲךָ֣ יְהוָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: I will wash mine hands in innocency: so will I compass thine altar, O LORD:
verses.rendering:
I wash my hands in innocence
and go around your altar, O LORD,
translator_notes: Erchats be-niqqayon kappai ('I wash in the cleanness of my hands') may refer to a literal ritual washing before approaching the altar (compare Exodus 30:17-21, where priests wash hands and feet before serving) or to a metaphorical declaration of innocence. The 'going around' (asovevah) the altar suggests a processional circuit — a liturgical act of worship performed physically, walking around the altar as a visible declaration of devotion.

verses.text_kjv: That I may publish with the voice of thanksgiving, and tell of all thy wondrous works.
verses.rendering:
raising a shout of thanksgiving
and telling of all your wondrous deeds.
translator_notes: Qol todah ('the voice/sound of thanksgiving') is the vocal component of the todah offering — the thanksgiving sacrifice accompanied by public declaration of what God has done. Nifleotekha ('your wonders, your extraordinary acts') are the specific deeds God has performed that merit telling. Worship in the Hebrew Bible is never abstract; it is always tied to concrete divine actions.

verses.text_hebrew: יְֽהוָ֗ה אָ֭הַבְתִּי מְע֣וֹן בֵּיתֶ֑ךָ וּ֝מְק֗וֹם מִשְׁכַּ֥ן כְּבוֹדֶֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: LORD, I have loved the habitation of thy house, and the place where thine honour dwelleth.
verses.rendering:
O LORD, I love the dwelling place of your house,
the place where your glory resides.

key_terms.semantic_range: glory, honor, weight, heaviness, radiance, splendor
key_terms.note: The glory (kavod) that fills the temple is God's visible, weighty presence. The speaker loves not the building but what the building contains — the tangible manifestation of God's presence among his people. Mishkan ('dwelling place, tabernacle') echoes the wilderness tabernacle where God's kavod first descended (Exodus 40:34).

translator_notes: The love (ahavti) for God's house is personal and passionate. This is the same verb used for marital love, for love between friends, for love of God. The speaker's attachment to the temple is emotional, not merely dutiful.

verses.text_hebrew: אַל־תֶּאֱסֹ֣ף עִם־חַטָּאִ֣ים נַפְשִׁ֑י וְעִם־אַנְשֵׁ֖י דָמִ֣ים חַיָּֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: Gather not my soul with sinners, nor my life with bloody men:
verses.rendering:
Do not sweep my soul away with sinners,
or my life with men of blood —
translator_notes: Al-teesof ('do not gather, do not sweep away') — the speaker fears being grouped with sinners in God's judgment. The separation he has practiced in life (vv. 4-5) he asks God to honor in judgment. Anshei damim ('men of blood') are those guilty of bloodshed — the most extreme category of wickedness. The speaker asks not to share their fate.

verses.text_kjv: In whose hands is mischief, and their right hand is full of bribes.
verses.rendering:
in whose hands is wickedness,
and whose right hand is full of bribes.
translator_notes: Zimmah ('wicked scheme, evil plan, lewdness') in their hands and shochad ('bribery') filling their right hand — the portrait is of systematic corruption. The right hand (yamin), the hand of power and action, is saturated with bribes. These are not occasional sinners but professional operators of injustice.

verses.text_kjv: But as for me, I will walk in mine integrity: redeem me, and be gracious unto me.
verses.rendering:
But as for me, I walk in my integrity.
Redeem me and be gracious to me.
translator_notes: The contrast is explicit: va-ani ('but as for me') sets the speaker apart from the corrupt. He returns to the opening claim — be-tummi elekh ('in my integrity I walk'). But the final two verbs are surprising: pedeni ('redeem me') and chonneni ('be gracious to me'). Even the person who walks in integrity needs redemption and grace. Integrity is not self-sufficiency. The most upright person still depends on God's intervention.

verses.text_hebrew: רַ֭גְלִי עָמְדָ֣ה בְמִישׁ֑וֹר בְּ֝מַקְהֵלִ֗ים אֲבָרֵ֥ךְ יְהוָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: My foot standeth in an even place: in the congregations will I bless the LORD.
verses.rendering:
My foot stands on level ground;
in the assemblies I will bless the LORD.
translator_notes: Ragli amdah ve-mishor ('my foot stands in a level place') means the speaker has found solid, even footing — not the treacherous ground of the wicked but the stable terrain of integrity. Mishor ('level ground, uprightness, plain') suggests both physical stability and moral straightness. The psalm closes in worship: be-maqhelim avarakh YHWH ('in the assemblies I will bless the LORD'). The speaker who refused the assembly of evildoers (v. 5) takes his place in the assembly of the faithful.
